narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early academic deciles, LSAT score, family income bracket, and planned full-time enrollment, and it calculated a 73% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their programs. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average decile of about 5.5—and signaled to the model a concerning drop or stagnation in performance later in your studies. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a 1 compared to the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing the model's view of consistently low relative standing in your school's rankings from the start. While your undergraduate GPA provided some counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing—being so close to the norm—it wasn't strong enough to offset those weaker academic decile signals. Your LSAT score of 31, notably below the 36.7 average, and lower-middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile stands out for those bottom-decile rankings, which are rarer among successful first-time passers and heavily weighed by the model. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, but rather how these metrics aligned with patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above the average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a full-time aspiring law student. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could significantly shift the model's outlook in your favor.
